The surgeon identifies the gastropexy suture, typically an ethebon suture, located on the patient's left side at the greater curvature of the stomach. This suture is observed pexing the stomach around the top of the band to prevent slippage.
The surgeon identifies and removes the ethebon suture on the greater curvature of the stomach, which was previously used to secure the band and prevent slippage.

The surgeon uses robotic scissors to carefully take down the gastropexy suture on the greater curvature of the stomach. The robotic camera provides a clear view of the anatomy, revealing the lap band free from surrounding adhesions and scar tissue.

The surgeon uses robotic scissors to carefully dissect and free the lap band from surrounding adhesions and scar tissue, with the robotic camera providing a clear view of the anatomical structures involved.

The surgeon uses a three-arm robotic technique to take down the vessels of the greater curvature of the stomach. A tip-up grasper is positioned in the fourth arm, a Cartier or pro grasp in the first arm, with the camera in the second arm, and a vessel sealer or scissors in the third arm.

The surgeon employs a three-arm robotic technique, utilizing a tip-up grasper in the fourth position, a Cartier or pro grasp in the first position, and a camera in the second position. The third arm is equipped with either a vessel sealer or scissors.

The surgeon uses a robotic vessel sealer to carefully dissect and take down the vessels along the greater curvature of the stomach, ensuring close proximity to the stomach to prevent bleeding and avoid accessory vessels.

The surgeon uses a robotic vessel sealer to carefully dissect and remove adhesions from the greater curvature of the stomach, staying close to the stomach to prevent bleeding and avoid accessory vessels.

The surgeon applies traction and counter traction to expose the surgical field. The lap band, initially obstructing bougie placement, is removed. The short gastric vessels are dissected, allowing for the subsequent placement of the bougie.

The surgeon applies traction and counter traction to expose the surgical field. The lap band is observed obstructing the placement of the bougie, prompting the decision to take down the short gastric vessels.
The surgeon places a bougie into the stomach after freeing the lap band, using the Vazigi bougie device, which features suction capabilities.

The surgeon loosens the lap band and places a bougie into the stomach. The lap band is then removed from the patient's stomach and set aside. The surgeon inspects the band for any brown discoloration, which could indicate a potential leak or erosion of the stomach.
The surgeon loosens the lap band from the patient's stomach, places a bougie inside, and then removes the band, setting it aside.

The surgeon closely examines the lap band for any brown discoloration, which may indicate a stomach leak or erosion. Adhesive bands are inspected and sometimes divided to ensure there is no significant stricture present.

The surgeon inspects adhesive bands around the esophagus, dividing them to prevent stricture. A circumferential dissection is performed to ensure clear anatomy. The bougie is placed, and the robotic stapler is prepared to initiate the sleeve gastrectomy procedure.

The surgeon performs circumferential dissection around the esophagus to inspect and ensure clear anatomy. The bougie is placed, and the robotic stapler is prepared to initiate the sleeve gastrectomy procedure.

The surgeon uses a green load on the robotic stapler to make the initial staple line on the stomach, assessing tissue thickness. After the first fire, the surgeon transitions to a blue load for subsequent stapling, as the stomach tissue is thickest in this area.

Usually I will use a fire of the green load. provide initial fire. I think the robotic technology that we have with these intuitive staplers is amazing. It'll tell you if the tissue is too thick. It will sense how long you need to hold pressure for. And they've really made this case quite enjoyable for me.

The surgeon fires the first staple line on the thickest part of the stomach using a green load, then transitions to a blue load for subsequent stapling. The staple line is carefully inspected for any interference that could affect future stapling.

The surgeon inspects the staple line on the stomach after firing to ensure no staples interfere with future stapling. The bougie is adjusted slightly, and the stapling continues proximally along the stomach.

The surgeon adjusts the position of the bougie within the stomach and continues stapling along the proximal stomach. The stapler is fired multiple times, with the number of firings varying based on the unique anatomy of each stomach.

The surgeon uses robotic arms to hold the stomach flat, aligning the stapler along the bougie for the final few staple fires. The area is cleaned to ensure no significant scarring interferes with stapling, confirming the stomach is healthy and viable.

The surgeon cleans the crotch of the staple line before firing an additional stapler. Using robotic arms, the stomach is held flat and returned to its normal anatomical position. The stapler is aligned to hug the bougie gently, ensuring no extra tension is applied as the stapler is activated.

The surgeon approaches the final staple fires near the gastroesophageal junction, ensuring the stomach is healthy and viable. Scar tissue is present but does not interfere with stapling. The staple line is cleared, and the stomach is positioned correctly to complete the staple line.
The surgeon focuses on the staple line at the gastroesophageal junction, ensuring the stomach is properly positioned and clearing the staple line before completing the final stapling.
The surgeon carefully aligns the stapler along the stomach, hugging the staple line close to the bougie to ensure minimal residual fundus remains, as the final staple fires are placed.

The surgeon completes the final staple fires along the stomach, ensuring the staple line closely hugs the bougie to avoid leaving excess fundus. The stomach is visibly separated and positioned aside, while anesthesia begins to remove the bougie.

The surgeon has completed the separation of the stomach and places it aside. The bougie is being removed by anesthesia. Observing a slight torsion of the stomach, the surgeon applies a vical suture to pexy the stomach to the peripancreatic fat, ensuring it lays flat without excessive tension to prevent twisting or narrowing.
The surgeon observes torsion in the stomach, indicating a potential corkscrew effect. To prevent retraction, a vical suture is placed to pexy the stomach to the surrounding peripancreatic fat.
The surgeon ensures the stomach lays flat with a smooth contour, avoiding any narrowing or torsion, which is crucial for the patient's future eating and swallowing function.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to suture the gastric sleeve, followed by an intraoperative gastroscopy to check for torsion or leaks and ensure smooth anatomy. Fibrin sealant is applied robotically to the staple line, and a portion of the patient's omentum is placed over it for additional hemostasis.
